title: Vital Questions to Ask Your Guidance Counselor About College
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have a meeting with your guidance counselor. You sit down. They ask how they can help. You say you want to go to a good college. They nod and offer some general advice about keeping your grades up and getting involved in extracurriculars. The meeting ends. You walk out feeling like nothing happened, because nothing did. The problem was not your counselor. It was your questions. Generic questions produce generic answers. Specific questions produce useful information. Here are the questions that turn a counselor meeting from a formality into a strategic planning session.

Questions About Your Academic Path

Do not ask whether your GPA is good enough for college. Ask: Based on my transcript and the colleges I am considering, are there specific courses I should take next year to strengthen my application? This question produces a specific answer tied to your actual record and goals. The counselor can point to gaps in your transcript, recommend advanced courses in your areas of strength, or suggest alternatives if a recommended course is not available.

Ask: Are there colleges that are particularly strong in my intended major that I might not have heard of? Students tend to apply to schools with famous names. Counselors know about lesser-known schools with excellent programs in specific fields. A student interested in marine biology might benefit from a small coastal college with research vessels that no one outside the field has heard of. The counselor can surface these options.

Ask: Based on students from our school with similar profiles to mine, which colleges should I be looking at? Your counselor has data on where previous students from your high school applied, were accepted, and enrolled. They know which colleges have historically been receptive to students with your GPA and course rigor. This information is more useful than national rankings because it is specific to your context.

Questions About Standardized Testing

Do not ask whether you should take the SAT or ACT. Ask: Based on my academic strengths, which test am I likely to perform better on, and when should I take it for the first time? The counselor can help you decide between the SAT and ACT based on your strengths in math, reading, and science. They can also recommend a testing timeline that gives you room for a retake if needed.

Ask: Do the colleges I am considering require test scores, or are they test-optional, and how should that affect my preparation? The test-optional landscape has changed significantly. Some schools are test-optional in name but still consider scores when submitted. Others are truly test-blind. Your counselor can help you understand which policy applies to your target schools and how to strategize accordingly.

Questions About Financial Aid and Scholarships

Do not ask whether college is affordable. Ask: What scholarships are available specifically to students from our school or our area, and what are the deadlines I need to know? Many communities have local scholarships through Rotary clubs, foundations, and alumni associations. These scholarships often have smaller applicant pools than national ones. Your counselor knows about them, or knows where to find them.

Ask: Based on my family's financial situation, which colleges on my list are likely to be affordable after financial aid? Counselors can help you understand the difference between a school with a high sticker price and generous need-based aid and a school with a lower sticker price and less aid. The net price, what you actually pay, is often very different from the published price.

Ask: What is the FAFSA timeline, and when should my family complete it to maximize aid eligibility? The FAFSA opens on a specific date each year. Some aid is distributed on a first-come basis. Filing early matters. Your counselor can tell you the optimal filing window.

Questions About the Application Process

Ask: When should I ask teachers for recommendation letters, and how should I approach them? The best time to ask is spring of junior year, not fall of senior year when every student is asking. Your counselor can advise on which teachers to ask based on your intended major and which teachers write the strongest letters. They can also tell you what information to provide your recommenders to help them write specifically about you.

Ask: Is there anything on my transcript or record that I should explain in the additional information section of my application? If you had a dip in grades during a particular semester, a disciplinary incident, or an unusual circumstance, your counselor can advise on whether and how to address it. The additional information section is not a place for excuses, but it is a place for context. Your counselor can help you strike the right tone.

Ask: What are the most common mistakes students from our school make on their applications? Counselors see the same errors year after year: essays that try to cover too much, activity lists that are padded with meaningless entries, applications to reach schools with no safety options. Your counselor can tell you what to avoid.

The Bottom Line

A guidance counselor meeting is only as valuable as the questions you bring to it. Do not ask for general advice. Ask for specific information tied to your transcript, your goals, and your context. Ask about academic planning, testing strategy, financial aid, and application pitfalls. The counselor has data and experience you cannot access on your own. Your job is to extract it with better questions.
